text:
This chapter employs a human-machine interaction interpretation method to accurately interpret the MSS remote sensing images from 1975, TM remote sensing images from 1990, and ETM remote sensing images from 2000 in the Songliao Plain, obtaining data on land salinization and alkalization for three periods. Through spatial overlay analysis and statistics of the interpreted data, the temporal and spatial changes in land salinization and alkalization in the Songliao Plain were identified, summarized as follows: 

1. In terms of the spatial distribution of saline-alkali soils, two characteristics are observed: first, saline-alkali soils are predominantly located around water systems such as rivers, lakes, and reservoirs; second, they are often found in border areas of administrative divisions. For example, the areas around Baicheng, Songyuan, and western Changchun on the west side of the Nenjiang River and the Second Songhua River; the northeastern part of Tongliao City at the border of Jilin and Inner Mongolia; the southern regions of the Hinggan League; and parts of Daqing, Suihua, and Qiqihar in Heilongjiang on the east side of the Nenjiang River and the Second Songhua River. Severe salinization is mainly distributed in Daqing, Zhaoyuan County, and the western part of Lanxi County on the west side of the Nenjiang and Second Songhua Rivers, as well as in the low-lying areas between Songyuan and the Second Songhua River, Baicheng, Tongyu County, and Qian'an Changling County in Songyuan, and western Changchun. The areas of light and moderate salinization are relatively large but are primarily contained within a parallelogram defined by the four endpoints of Fuyu County in Qiqihar, Lanxi County in Suihua, Jiergalang Town in Kezuo Houqi of Tongliao, and Chabugait in Zhalute Banner of Tongliao.

2. Regarding the area changes of saline-alkali soils, the total area in 1975 was 19,042.13 km², increasing to 23,825.87 km² in 1990 and 24,925.94 km² in 2000, indicating an overall upward trend. The area of severely salinized land increased rapidly, from 6.79% of the total saline-alkali land in 1975 to 13.82% in 1990 and 19.12% in 2000. The area of moderately salinized land showed little change in the first 15 years of the study period, with a slight increase but a decrease in its proportion of the total saline-alkali land (32.99% in 1975, 31.66% in 1990). In the subsequent 11 years, the area increased rapidly, with its proportion rising by nearly 6 percentage points. The area of lightly salinized land exhibited a pattern of first increasing and then decreasing, with its percentage of the total area showing a monotonically declining trend.

3. In terms of the rate of change of saline-alkali soils, from 1975 to 1990, the rate of change for severely salinized land was the fastest, increasing at an annual rate of 10.31%, while lightly salinized land had the slowest rate of change at 0.88%, and moderately salinized land increased at a rate of 1.34% per year. From 1990 to 2000, the fastest change was still in severely salinized land, which, although its rate of increase declined, still grew at an annual rate of 3.04%. Lightly salinized land continued to decrease at a rate of 1.46% per year, achieving negative growth.

4. From the perspective of the temporal and spatial dynamic changes of saline-alkali soils, between 1975 and 1990, the area of land with reversal and strong reversal of salinization was 7,565.6 km², while the area of deterioration and severe deterioration was 15,982.5 km², indicating an upward and expanding trend in salinization in terms of both degree and area, leading to ecological degradation. From 1990 to 2000, the area of land with reversal and strong reversal was 8,456.9 km², and the area of deterioration and severe deterioration was 10,889.2 km², indicating that the deterioration trend of salinization during this period was effectively controlled, with strong reversal forces, and the overall desertification situation in the region improved. 

From the spatial dynamic changes of saline-alkali soils, between 1975 and 1990, the deterioration and severe deterioration phenomena in the marginal areas of salinization were stronger than in the hinterland, with more pronounced deterioration around administrative boundaries and near lakes and marshes, primarily distributed in most areas of Baicheng, Daqing, and Songyuan, as well as southern Qiqihar and southwestern Suihua. The distribution of reversal types in the study area was relatively scattered, mainly concentrated in southern Baicheng and southwestern Songyuan. Strong reversal types were only sporadically distributed, mainly around lakes and marshes. This was related to the fact that the region experienced a year of abundant water around 1990, with rising water levels in lakes and marshes submerging surrounding saline-alkali land. Between 1990 and 2000, the area of reversal and strong reversal was relatively large, with distribution locations somewhat coinciding with those of the deterioration types from 1975 to 1990, indicating that reversal phenomena were stronger in the surrounding areas of salinization than in the hinterland, occurring more frequently around lakes and marshes or administrative boundaries. The area of strong reversal phenomena was relatively small, but the range of reversal types was broader, mainly distributed in northeastern Daqing, western Suihua, along the Yin River, Lu River, and Nenjiang River in Qiqihar, as well as in the western part of Baicheng and the border areas with Kezuo Yuyouqi and Tuquan County, and in Changling County of Songyuan. Severe deterioration was only sporadically distributed, relatively concentrated along the Lu River and at the border between Daqing and Anda. The area of deterioration types was larger, mainly concentrated around rivers, lakes, and administrative boundaries, with the main distribution still in Daqing, Suihua, Qiqihar in Heilongjiang, and Baicheng and Songyuan in Jilin, surrounding the severe deterioration types.

1. Chemical analysis of soil samples in the study area revealed that the dominant cation is Na+ and the predominant anion is HCO3-, indicating that the main composition of soil salinity in the area is sodium bicarbonate (NaHCO3). Statistical analysis of salinity ions showed a strong correlation between Na+ content and electrical conductivity (EC), with a correlation coefficient of R2=0.987; however, there was no significant correlation between HCO3- content and EC. 

2. Analysis of the spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soil in the study area using ASTER remote sensing imagery found reflection peaks in bands 4 and 6, strong absorption valleys in bands 5 and 7, and a weak absorption valley in band 3. This "three valleys and two peaks" spectral curve characteristic is very helpful for identifying saline-alkali soil. 

3. When studying the spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soil in the imagery, various transformations of reflectance were applied. Practical results showed that these transformations enhanced the spectral differences between reflectance curves, making it easier to identify inflection points in reflectance, which improved the correlation between soil salinity and spectral transformation values.

4. Statistical analysis of soil salinity with reflectance and its transformed forms revealed that the transformation of reflectance helps improve its correlation with salinity. Among these, the logarithmic transformation of reflectance increased the determination coefficient R2 from 0.507 to 0.518.

5. The differential form of reflectance and its transformations significantly aided the correlation with soil salinity. Research indicated a strong correlation between the first derivative of the logarithm of reflectance and salinity, with a correlation coefficient of R=0.826 and RMSE=1.893.

6. A multiple regression model can reflect the role of multiple bands in the estimation process, enhancing inversion accuracy. The study introduced three independent variables to establish a regression model: the reflectance of band 1 (R1), the logarithm of the reflectance of band 3 (logR3), and the first derivative of the logarithm of the reflectance of band 4 ((logR4)'); the model is expressed as: Y= -66.512 (logR4)' + 40.19 logR3 -16.67 R1 + 35.924. The model's validation results showed a determination coefficient of R2=0.881 and RMSE=1.186, achieving good inversion results and meeting the expected goals.

7. The study explored several classification methods, including maximum likelihood classification, BP neural network classification, and decision tree classification. Validation of the three classification results indicated the following accuracies: decision tree (overall classification accuracy 86.7%) > BP neural network (85.3%) > maximum likelihood classification (81.8%). Therefore, the decision tree method was ultimately adopted for classifying typical features and extracting saline-alkali soil in the study area, yielding satisfactory classification results.

1. This study analyzes the spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soil in the Songliao Plain using TM remote sensing imagery. It was found that the spectral curve shapes and trends of saline-alkali soils with different salt contents are generally similar, exhibiting lower reflectance in bands 1 to 3, with a weak absorption valley occurring in band 3. The reflectance rapidly increases from bands 3 to 5, reaching a peak in band 5. 

2. A statistical analysis of the relationship between salt content and reflectance revealed that mathematical transformations of reflectance can enhance its correlation with salt content to varying degrees. Specifically, applying a logarithmic transformation to band 5 resulted in a correlation coefficient of R²=0.755 and RMSE=1.699 with salt content.

3. The differential form of reflectance and its variations can also improve the correlation with salt content to some extent. The study shows that the first derivative transformation of the logarithm of reflectance in band 6 helps enhance the correlation with salt content, achieving a coefficient of determination of R²=0.763 and RMSE=1.713.

4. This research established a multiple linear regression model using three independent variables: reflectance in band 5, its reciprocal, and the first derivative of the logarithm of reflectance in band 6: Y=27.255R5 + 52.319(logR6)' - 0.394*(1/R5) + 3.683. The model has a coefficient of determination R²=0.834 and a root mean square error RMSE=1.386, indicating satisfactory inversion results and achieving the expected goals.

1. This section first introduces the satellite parameters and data characteristics of the resource environment satellite HJ-1-A, and performs geometric correction, atmospheric correction, and other preprocessing on the HSI data within the study area.  
2. An analysis of the radiance spectral curves of HSI hyperspectral data in the study area reveals that the curve shapes of different land covers are similar, with strong absorption valleys at 760nm and 820nm, indicating that the spectral curves of HSI radiance products are greatly influenced by the atmosphere, making it difficult to reflect the true physical characteristics of the land cover if applied directly.  
3. This section utilizes the FLSAAH atmospheric correction model to perform atmospheric correction on HSI radiance data. The corrected surface reflectance spectral curves show no significant atmospheric and water vapor absorption features, better reflecting the true spectral characteristics of the land cover. After atmospheric correction, the spectral curve differences between different land covers are more pronounced, making it easier to distinguish and classify them.  
4. When studying the spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soil in HSI hyperspectral images, transformations such as logarithm, reciprocal, and first derivative were applied to the reflectance. Correlation analysis between the measured salt content at each sampling point and the corresponding reflectance (and its transformed forms) revealed that the transformations enhanced the spectral differences between the curves, making it easier to identify inflection points and improving the correlation between soil salt content and spectral transformation values.  
5. The correlation coefficients between soil salt content and reflectance and its transformed forms showed that the correlations improved after applying logarithmic, reciprocal, and first derivative transformations to the reflectance. The order of the model's determination coefficients was: (1/R)’ > R’ > logR > 1/logR > R > 1/R > (1/logR)’ > (logR)’. The determination coefficient R² for reflectance (R) increased from 0.498 to 0.555 for the first derivative of the reciprocal reflectance (1/R)’.  
6. This paper introduces the Partial Least Squares Regression (PLSR) method to study the relationship between soil salt content and HSI image reflectance and its transformed forms. PLSR reduces the dimensionality of spectral data while maximizing information content, thus improving analysis efficiency. The results indicate that the predictive model established using 1 to 3 principal components extracted by PLSR significantly outperforms single-band correlation analysis methods, with the determination coefficient for the first derivative of reflectance reaching 0.851.  
7. This study investigates the predictive relationships between eight salt ions in saline-alkali soil, soil pH, EC values, and HSI image reflectance. The results show that the inversion model established using PLSR performs well in predicting Na+, K+, HCO3-, Cl-, and EC, with the determination coefficient for the EC prediction model reaching 0.741.  
8. Based on the spectral characteristics of different land covers and combined with techniques such as Principal Component Analysis (PCA), Minimum Noise Fraction (MNF), and Pure Pixel Index (PPI), satisfactory results were achieved in classifying saline-alkali soil.

1. Statistical analysis of the two inversion results shows that from 1990 to 2002, the area of salinized and alkaline land increased at a rate of 97.57 km² per year, with a net increase of 1170.87 km² over 12 years. The area of mildly salinized land decreased by 1058 km², accounting for 10.56% of the salinized land, while moderately and severely salinized land increased at rates of 51.12 km²/year and 103.25 km²/year, with net increases of 613.95 km² and 1239.02 km², respectively, over the 12 years. 

2. Examination of the transfer matrix of various landscape types reveals: (1) For severely salinized land: The area converted from other land types to severely salinized land was 2147.08 km², mainly sourced from moderately and mildly salinized land; the area converted from severely salinized land to other types was 827.86 km², primarily to saline land, moderately salinized land, and mildly salinized land. (2) For moderately salinized land: The area converted from other types to moderately salinized land was 3559.811 km², mainly from mildly salinized and non-degraded land, with mildly salinized land accounting for 58.83% of the total area. The area of moderately salinized land converted to other types was 2987.319 km², primarily to severely salinized and mildly salinized land. (3) For mildly salinized land: During the study period, the area converted from other types to mildly salinized land was 2959.936 km², mainly from moderately salinized land, accounting for 54.48% of the total area; the area of mildly salinized land converted to other types was 4060.708 km², significantly higher than the area converted from other types to mildly salinized land, indicating a clear reduction in the area of mildly salinized land during this period.

3. Analyzing the rate of change in the area of each landscape type over the 12 years, the data shows that the fastest increasing type was saline land, with an annual growth rate of 17.77%; followed by severely salinized land at 5.88% per year, and then moderately salinized land. Mildly salinized land, however, decreased at an annual rate of 1.2%.

4. From a landscape-level perspective, the overall trend of landscape types in the study area from 1975 to 2002 shows an increase in the number of patches, while the average patch area decreased, and the index of spreading decreased, indicating that the landscape in the study area is generally becoming more fragmented. The increase in fractal dimension suggests that the shapes of patches within the study area are becoming increasingly complex and irregular. The increase in diversity and evenness indices indicates that the landscape types within the study area are moving towards diversification and uniformity.

5. From the perspective of landscape indices at the type level, the number of patches and patch density for severely, moderately, and mildly salinized land showed an increasing trend each year, while the average patch area decreased annually, indicating that these three types of salinized landscapes are trending towards fragmentation. Mildly salinized land consistently had the largest average patch area, indicating that it has remained the dominant landscape type in the area; boundary density increased each year, suggesting that the shapes of the three types of salinized land patches are becoming more complex and irregular, significantly influenced by human activities. Among them, the boundary density of mildly salinized land remained relatively high, indicating its dominant position until 2002, when it became comparable to that of moderately salinized land, at which point both had similar dominance. The increase in fractal dimension indicates that the shapes of the three landscape patches are all becoming more complex, a conclusion also supported by the results of the area-weighted average shape index. Around 1975, the aggregation indices for moderately and mildly salinized land types were relatively low, indicating that these two types of patches were predominantly adjacent to similar patch types. This was due to the small area of severely and moderately salinized land at that time, resulting in a relatively homogeneous patch type within the study area. However, with the increasing frequency of human activities and the intensification of land salinization, the adjacency probability among various land types gradually increased.

6. Overall, the fragmentation degree of severely, moderately, and mildly salinized land in the Songliao Plain has increased, with area gradually increasing and patches becoming more fragmented. The fragmentation degree of water bodies showed an upward trend in the first 15 years, while in the following 12 years, it stabilized and even moved towards simplification and regularization. This change hinders the flow of materials and energy between landscape units, affecting the stability of landscape patterns, leading to the degradation of ecosystem functions, and resulting in ecological environmental degradation. This is primarily due to the weak ecological foundation of the Songliao Plain, along with factors such as population growth, economic backwardness, and extensive production methods.

This article focuses on the Songliao Plain as the research area, utilizing multi-source remote sensing data to study the land salinization and alkalization status over the past 30 years. It interprets MSS image data from 1975, TM image data from 1990, and ETM image data from 2000 through human-computer interaction, and employs geographic information systems for spatial analysis. The study also reverses the soil salinity in the research area using ASTER remote sensing images from 2004, TM remote sensing images from around 1990, and soil sampling analysis data. Using landscape ecology theories and methods, it analyzes the changes in the landscape pattern of saline-alkali soils over the past 30 years. The following conclusions are drawn: 

1. The remote sensing interpretation results for the three periods of saline-alkali soil show that the total area of saline-alkali land was 19,042.13 km² in 1975, 23,825.87 km² in 1990, and 24,925.94 km² in 2000, indicating an overall upward trend. Among these, severe salinization increased the fastest, with an annual growth rate of 10.31% from 1975 to 1990, and although the growth rate slowed to 3.04% per year from 1990 to 2000, it still continued to rise. Mild salinization grew the slowest, increasing by 0.88% annually in the earlier period and decreasing by 1.46% annually in the later period. Using geographic information systems for overlay analysis of the interpretation results, it was found that from 1975 to 1990, the area of land experiencing reversal and strong reversal of salinization was 7,565.6 km², while the area of deterioration and strong deterioration was 15,982.5 km², indicating an increase and expansion trend in salinization in terms of both degree and area during this period, leading to ecological degradation. From 1990 to 2000, the area of land experiencing reversal and strong reversal of salinization was 8,456.9 km², and the area of deterioration and strong deterioration was 10,889.2 km², suggesting that the deterioration trend of salinization in the research area during this period was largely controlled, with strong reversal forces.

2. The 6S model was used to perform atmospheric correction on the ASTER remote sensing images covering the research area, analyzing the spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soils in the ASTER images. Saline-alkali soils exhibited reflection peaks in the 4th and 6th bands, with strong absorption valleys in the 5th and 7th bands, and a weak absorption valley in the 3rd band. This spectral curve characteristic of three valleys and two peaks can effectively distinguish saline-alkali soils.

3. After applying mathematical transformations and differential transformations to the reflectance, the correlation with salinity could be improved to varying degrees. A multiple regression model was ultimately established to reverse the soil salinity in the research area, represented by the model: Y = -66.512(logR4)' + 40.19logR3 - 16.67R1 + 35.924, with a determination coefficient R² = 0.881 and a root mean square error RMSE = 1.186.

4. The spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soils in TM remote sensing images were analyzed: the reflectance of saline-alkali soils in the first three bands of TM images was very low, while it significantly increased in the near-infrared and short-wave infrared bands, with a reflection peak appearing in the 5th band. A multiple regression model was established based on the combined reflectance of bands, reflectance transformation forms, and differential forms: Y = 27.255R5 + 52.319(logR6)' - 0.394*1/R + 3.683, with a determination coefficient R² = 0.834 and a root mean square error RMSE = 1.386.

5. The spectral characteristics of saline-alkali soils in HSI hyperspectral images were analyzed: in the radiance images, saline-alkali soils, like other land types, showed two distinct valleys around 760 nm and 820 nm due to the influence of oxygen and water vapor, with similar spectral curve shapes across various land types. After atmospheric correction, the spectral curves of the land types were smoothed, showing no significant water vapor or atmospheric absorption features. Correlation analysis between the single-band reflectance of HSI images and the measured salinity of sampled soils revealed that reflectance, after logarithmic, reciprocal, and first-order differential transformations, could improve the correlation with salinity to varying degrees, with the first-order differential of the reciprocal showing the best effect, achieving a correlation coefficient of 0.744 around 780 nm. To enhance inversion accuracy and eliminate the effects of linear correlations between hyperspectral bands, partial least squares regression analysis (PLSR) was employed to extract several principal components from the hyperspectral data that could maximally explain variations in salinity content, used for quantitatively reversing the total salt content, salt ion content, EC value, and pH value of the soil.

6. A Markov transition matrix was established using the inversion results, and the statistical results indicated that during the study period, the area of other types of land converted to severe salinization was 2,147.08 km², with contributions from moderate salinization, mild salinization, water bodies, and non-degraded land at rates of 48.51%, 28.85%, 13.49%, and 8.04%, respectively. The area converted from severe salinization to other types was 827.86 km², including saline land, moderate salinization, mild salinization, non-degraded land, and water bodies, accounting for 18.42%, 36.53%, 36.45%, 5.39%, and 3.21% of the total area, respectively. The area converted from other types to moderate salinization was 3,559.811 km², mainly from mild salinization and non-degraded land, with contribution rates of 58.83% and 22.86%. The area of moderate salinization converted to other land types was 2,987.319 km², primarily converting to severe and mild salinization, with probabilities of 34.86% and 53.98%. The area of mild salinization converted to other types of saline-alkali land was 2,959.936 km², with 54.48% coming from moderate salinization, while the area converted to other land types was 4,060.708 km², with 51.57% of that area converting to moderate salinization land.

7. Landscape pattern indices were used to quantitatively analyze the spatial pattern of saline-alkali land in the Songliao Plain. From an overall landscape perspective, the number of patches in the research area increased annually, the average patch area decreased, the fractal dimension increased, and patches tended to become more fragmented, complex, and irregular. The diversity index and evenness index increased, indicating a trend towards homogenization and diversification of landscape types in the research area. At the type level, the number of patches, patch density, and boundary density of the three types of saline-alkali soils increased annually during the study period, with all landscape patches tending towards fragmentation and shapes gradually becoming more complex and irregular. The increase in fractal dimension and area-weighted average shape index also corroborated this viewpoint. In the later period, the boundary density of mild salinization land showed little change, and the shape changes tended to stabilize. Throughout the study period, the boundary density of mild salinization land remained at a relatively high value, indicating that mild salinization land maintained a dominant position. Water bodies showed a rapid increase in patch fragmentation during the first 15 years, while the fragmentation rate stabilized in the subsequent 12 years, even developing towards a simpler and more regular form.

The area of saline-alkali land in Tongyu County has significantly increased, exhibiting a phased evolution, with no obvious changes in spatial patterns, although the scale differences in space are narrowing. Over the past 50 years, the total area of saline-alkali land in Tongyu County has notably expanded, but the salinization of land has undergone a process from rapid deterioration to gradual improvement. In terms of dynamics and the absolute values of annual area changes, the intensity of change in saline-alkali land was greater and the rate of change faster in the first 40 years. Furthermore, from 1965 to 2005, the area of saline-alkali land in various townships of Tongyu County increased, with varying degrees of dominance, leading to more complex patch shapes and increased differences in patch sizes; however, from 2005 to 2015, the area of saline-alkali land in each township decreased to varying extents, indicating some improvement in land salinization issues, with relatively minor changes in the landscape characteristics of saline-alkali land. At the township scale, the intensity and speed of dynamic evolution of saline-alkali land show significant regional differences. Specifically, townships with more severe land salinization experienced faster expansion of saline-alkali land, while those with lighter salinization saw greater intensity of expansion. In 1965, saline-alkali land in Tongyu County was mainly distributed in the northeastern region, particularly in Shihuadao Township, Hongxing Town, Shuanggang Town, and Baimian Township, while the southeastern area had relatively smaller saline-alkali land. Over the past 50 years, overall land salinization has intensified, with the worsening of salinization in densely distributed saline-alkali areas occurring simultaneously with the increase in saline-alkali land in areas with lighter salinization; however, the spatial pattern of more saline-alkali land in the northeast and less in the southwest has not changed significantly. Nevertheless, as the dynamic evolution of saline-alkali land in Tongyu County continues, the spatial scale differences are gradually narrowing. Factors such as reduced precipitation, rising temperatures, water resource shortages, and high-intensity human activities can exacerbate land salinization, but the effects of these factors exhibit a certain degree of scale dependence.

There exists a certain spatial coupling relationship between the evolution of rural residential land use and land salinization, but this relationship is constrained by the degree of land salinization. In areas where salinization is severe, the conflict between humans and land is more pronounced, and there is a strong spatial correlation between the evolution of rural residential land use and land salinization. Conversely, in areas with milder salinization, there is more flexibility in land use, and factors such as transportation and location have a stronger influence on rural residential land use. Therefore, the expansion and reduction of rural residential land use show little spatial correlation with land salinization. The expansion of rural residential land occupies nearby arable land and grassland, leading to the conversion of arable soil layers into construction land, which diminishes the ecosystem service value. Additionally, due to increased intensity of human activities, land salinization around rural residential areas intensifies, resulting in decreased land productivity and leading to phenomena such as land abandonment and the abandonment of rural residential land. Coupled with the growing population pressure, the conflict between humans and land becomes increasingly prominent. When available land resources are relatively scarce, population pressure forces humans to over-exploit land resources or utilize land in areas with poorer natural conditions. In regions where land salinization is severe, the ecological environment is more fragile, and available land resources are scarce, making it easy to lead to over-exploitation of land, which in turn exacerbates land degradation, creating a vicious cycle. Therefore, it is necessary to clarify the interaction between human activities and land degradation, optimize the spatial pattern of rural residential land use in ecologically fragile areas, and improve the ecological environment to achieve sustainable use of land resources.

The distribution pattern of saline-alkali land in the Huyang Lake Basin is as follows: 1) The main types of shallow soil salinization in the Huyang Lake Basin are sodium-alkaline salinization and sulfate saline soil. Saline-alkali soils can be divided into mild, moderate, and severe zones. The mild zone is located at the edge of the basin, with total salt content generally between 0.1% and 0.2%. The chemical types are primarily HCO3–Na and HCO3·SO4–Na, with thin layers of salt crust on the surface, which somewhat inhibit plant growth. The moderate zone is located inward from the mild zone, with total salt content generally between 0.2% and 0.4%. The chemical types include SO4·HCO3–Na, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, HCO3·SO4–Na, HCO3·SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, SO4·Cl–Na, and Cl·SO4–Na, with salt-alkali spots and thicker salt crusts appearing on the surface, leading to more significant inhibition of plant growth. The severe zone is found in the central area of the basin, with total salt content often exceeding 0.4% (many areas over 0.6%). The chemical types include SO4·HCO3–Na, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, and SO4–Na, severely inhibiting plant growth and making it difficult for crops to thrive. The dominant soil chemical types are Cl·SO4, SO4·Cl, and SO4·HCO3, corresponding to areas of shallow groundwater and salinized soil distribution; the SO4·HCO3 type is mainly distributed in the northern part of the study area, consistent with the distribution of non-salinized soils. From the perspective of formation causes, the mild and moderate zones are primarily due to irrigation raising the groundwater level, leading to salinization under strong evaporation (precipitation/irrigation – evaporation forming salts); the severe zone is closely related to intense groundwater evaporation and corresponds to saline water distribution areas, indicating that the degree of harm is directly related to low terrain, shallow groundwater, high salinity, and weak horizontal replenishment and drainage (bedrock – evaporation forming salts). 2) The chemical types of groundwater are well consistent with those of the soil. The water chemical types in the Huyang Lake Basin are not complex, showing slight horizontal zonation and no obvious vertical zonation, with chemical components dominated by SO4^2-, Cl^-, and Na^+. The northern part is mainly HCO3·SO4–Na, while the southwestern and eastern areas are HCO3–Na. South of the lowland, HCO3·SO4–Na·Mg, HCO3·Cl–Na, and SO4·Cl–Na·Mg types occasionally appear. The basin edge is primarily HCO3–Na and HCO3·SO4–Na; the inner edge is dominated by HCO3·SO4–Na·Mg, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, HCO3·Cl–Na, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, and SO4–Na·Mg; the central area is dominated by SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, SO4–Na, SO4·HCO3–Na·Mg, and SO4·Cl–Na. When the groundwater depth is less than the critical depth for effective evaporation, strong evaporation causes moisture loss and salt retention in the soil, resulting in good consistency between groundwater and soil chemical types. 3) The distribution of saline-alkali soils and changes in soil salinity are well consistent with groundwater depth. A high groundwater level above the critical water level and arid climate are among the conditions for salinization. Huyang Lake is located in a semi-arid zone, where strong evaporation leads to heavier salt accumulation and more severe salinization with decreasing depth, which is unfavorable for crop growth. 4) The distribution of saline-alkali soils corresponds to the degree of groundwater mineralization: monitoring indicates that areas with higher groundwater mineralization also exhibit greater soil salinization. 5) The severity of salinization is consistent with the topographical morphology. The Weinan Huyang Lake Basin features an open and gentle terrain, with higher surroundings and a lower center, forming a double-ring closed depression; the distribution of soil salinity also presents a closed ring centered around wetlands and lakes. Since the degree of salinization is consistent with groundwater depth, and the trend of groundwater levels is similar to the topographical trend, it ultimately presents a closed "double-ring" spatial pattern.

1) The formation conditions of saline-alkali land in the Huyang Lake Basin include both natural conditions and human factors. Natural conditions encompass topography, regional climate, soil environment, and hydrogeology; human factors mainly involve the impacts of irrigation and farming management. The Huyang Lake Basin is located in a semi-arid climate zone, with varying depths of groundwater, making it prone to salt accumulation under intense evaporation. Additionally, the basin is characterized by higher elevations surrounding a lower central area, which contributes to poor drainage and is a significant factor in the formation of saline-alkali land.

2) The distribution pattern of saline-alkali land in Huyang Lake is closely related to topography, groundwater levels, and the chemical types of groundwater and soil. Saline-alkali soils are categorized into light, moderate, and heavy zones: the light and moderate zones are associated with precipitation/irrigation-evaporation salt formation, while the heavy zone is linked to bedrock-evaporation salt formation. The light zone is located at the basin's edge, with a total salt content of 0.1–0.2%, predominantly HCO3–Na and HCO3·SO4–Na chemical types; the moderate zone lies within the light zone, with total salt content of 0.2–0.4%, including SO4·HCO3–Na, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, HCO3·SO4–Na, HCO3·SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, SO4·Cl–Na, and Cl·SO4–Na types; the heavy zone is situated in the basin's center, with total salt content often exceeding 0.4% (some exceeding 0.6%), characterized by SO4·HCO3–Na, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, and SO4–Na types. The groundwater chemical types in the Huyang Lake Basin show good consistency with the soil chemical types. The groundwater types are not complex, with slight horizontal zonation and no significant vertical zonation, primarily consisting of SO4^2-, Cl^-, and Na^+. The northern region is dominated by HCO3·SO4–Na, while the southwestern and eastern areas are characterized by HCO3–Na. In the southern edge of the depression, types such as HCO3·SO4–Na·Mg, HCO3·Cl–Na, and SO4·Cl–Na·Mg appear; the basin's edge is mainly composed of HCO3–Na and HCO3·SO4–Na; the inner edge features HCO3·SO4–Na·Mg, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, HCO3·Cl–Na, SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, and SO4–Na·Mg; the central area is primarily characterized by SO4·Cl–Na·Mg, SO4–Na, SO4·HCO3–Na·Mg, and SO4·Cl–Na.

3) The distribution of saline-alkali soils and variations in soil salinity are well correlated with groundwater depth, groundwater mineralization, and the topographical features of the basin. When the groundwater level exceeds the critical level and the climate is arid, evaporation causes groundwater depth and mineralization to directly influence soil salinity, thereby determining the degree of salinization. The severity of salinization in soils aligns with the topography of Huyang Lake, presenting an overall closed double-ring pattern.

4) The management of saline-alkali land in Huyang Lake requires a comprehensive approach that integrates water conservancy projects, agricultural practices, and biological measures. Water conservancy measures include the excavation of drainage channels and the development of wetlands to lower groundwater levels and control soil salinization. Building on this, a combination of agricultural and biological measures that promote the comprehensive development of agriculture, forestry, animal husbandry, sideline production, and fishery aims to achieve the goals of flood prevention and salinity control.

Land is a renewable natural resource that plays a crucial role in the material and energy cycles, serving as a valuable resource for human social and economic activities and survival. The salinization and alkalization of land lead to desolation and environmental degradation, posing a serious threat to human existence and development, and its research importance has become a global consensus. This paper combines remote sensing and geographic information technology, using MODIS remote sensing images as data sources along with regional relevant information, to monitor the dynamic changes of land salinization and alkalization in the study area, resulting in the following conclusions: 1. MODIS medium-resolution remote sensing data features high temporal resolution, extensive imaging coverage, and convenient data acquisition, making it suitable for dynamic monitoring of land salinization and alkalization at a regional scale, facilitating the acquisition of synchronous information and reducing data processing volume. 2. Preprocessing of three phases of MODIS images was conducted using ENVI software, while ERDAS software was utilized for remote sensing image classification and recognition, and land salinization and alkalization information was extracted through ARCGIS spatial analysis, effectively improving the accuracy of data extraction. 3. From a total trend analysis, the area of salinized and alkalized land in the study area increased from 8,350.68 km² in 2005 to 9,473.44 km² in 2011, showing an annual growth trend. The proportion of severely salinized and alkalized land in the total area for the three periods was 6.09%, 7.84%, and 8.62%, indicating that the degree of land salinization and alkalization in this area is still worsening, leading to further ecological deterioration. 4. Analyzing the rate of change in salinized and alkalized land, the total dynamic degree of salinized land from 2005 to 2008 was 4.36%, and from 2008 to 2011, it was 0.10%, indicating a slowdown in the growth rate of total salinized land area; the annual growth rate of severely salinized land from 2005 to 2011 was 4.16%, significantly higher than the average growth rate of total salinized land at 2.24%, suggesting that severely salinized land continues to worsen. 5. From the spatial conversion analysis, there is a diverse change characteristic among various land types, with frequent and large-area spatial conversions, indicating that the dynamic evolution of salinized land is distinctly complex. 6. From the spatial distribution analysis, salinized land in the study area is mainly concentrated in the three counties of Daqing, Dumeng, and Zhaoyuan, which together account for over 60% of the total salinized land area, with more than 70% of severely salinized land distributed within this range. 7. A Markov prediction model was established using 2011 as the starting point to forecast the development status of land salinization and alkalization in the study area. The results indicate that although the area of salinized land has decreased, it remains negligible compared to the total area, while severely salinized land continues to show a growth trend, indicating that the improvement and management of salinized land still face significant challenges.

This chapter conducts a correlational analysis of the parameters related to land salinization and soluble salts in Tongyu County based on the test data of surface soil, utilizing SPSS statistical techniques. The analysis is grounded in the salinization characteristic parameters—total salt content, pH, alkalinity, residual sodium carbonate, and sodium adsorption ratio—examining their relationship with the variations in the main soluble salt content in the soil. The results indicate that total salt content has a significant positive correlation with Cl⁻, HCO3⁻, and Na⁺; pH shows a significant positive correlation with CO3²⁻ and Na⁺; alkalinity is significantly positively correlated with Cl⁻, SO4²⁻, and Na⁺; and both residual sodium carbonate and sodium adsorption ratio exhibit significant positive correlations with CO3²⁻, SO4²⁻, and Na⁺. This means that as the residual sodium carbonate and sodium adsorption ratio increase, the concentrations of CO3²⁻, SO4²⁻, and Na⁺ in the soil also increase correspondingly. The analysis results employ a two-tailed significance hypothesis test, with a confidence level of 95%–99%. Based on the correlational analysis results, the main soluble salt indicators significantly related to land salinization parameters are identified as CO3²⁻, HCO3⁻, and Na⁺, laying the groundwork for subsequent research.

This chapter analyzes the dynamic changes of saline-alkali land in conjunction with the land use/land cover change study in Tongyu County from Chapter Four. The research results indicate that, in terms of quantitative changes, the area of saline-alkali land significantly increased from 1989 to 2000; however, from 2000 to 2004, the rate of increase slowed down, and the area decreased. In terms of land use type conversion, the saline-alkali land in this region mainly originates from the degradation of grasslands and the shrinkage of water bodies and wetlands. Regarding spatial distribution changes, the overall trend of land use intensity is higher in the east than in the west, higher in the south than in the north, and higher in the plains than in the low-lying areas. Based on the relevant analysis in Chapter Five, the Surfer software was used to create contour maps of the characteristic parameters affecting land salinization and the main soluble salts significantly correlated with it, analyzing the spatial distribution patterns of these eight indicators. Using ARCGIS software, the characteristic parameters of land salinization—total salt content, pH, alkalinity, residual sodium carbonate, and sodium adsorption ratio—along with the main soluble salts HCO3⁻, CO3²⁻, and Na⁺, a total of eight indicators, were spatially integrated to synthesize a spatial distribution map of land salinization in Tongyu County, analyzing the spatial distribution patterns of land salinization. Additionally, a brief analysis of the vertical distribution pattern of land salinization was conducted, revealing that the degree of salinization gradually intensifies from west to east, with severe salinization levels observed in the northern areas of Sijingzi and Shuanggang, as well as in most southern townships.

This chapter combines the previous research findings to provide a detailed analysis of the geological, climatic, and socio-economic factors influencing the salinization and alkalization of land in Tongyu County. It employs GIS spatial data overlay technology to analyze the salinized and alkalized land interpretation maps from 1989, 2000, and 2004 in conjunction with soil and landform type maps, exploring the relationship between environmental characteristics and the distribution of land salinization. The results indicate that salinized and alkalized land is primarily distributed in soil units such as dark calcareous soil, saline soil, alkaline soil, and sandy soil. Field investigations reveal that salinized and alkalized soils are mostly found in the low-lying areas between sand ridges, in water collection areas, and around waterlogged spots, mainly concentrated in geomorphological units like river lowlands, river floodplains, and lake accumulation plains. Overall, the formation process of land salinization and alkalization in Tongyu County is a comprehensive result of human irrational activities superimposed on a fragile ecological geological environment. The weak ecological geological conditions provide an objective basis for the occurrence of land salinization, while human irrational economic activities trigger and accelerate the process of land salinization.

(1) The importance of studying land salinization is elaborated from aspects such as the definitions of LUCC and land salinization, as well as the progress of domestic and international research. Additionally, the principles and applications of "3S" technology, SPSS software, and Surfer software are introduced. The summary of research progress and technical methods both clarifies the current status of land salinization research, the existing problems, and the development directions, while also providing a theoretical foundation and technical support for this study on the spatial distribution patterns of land salinization. (2) Using "3S" technology to extract land use/land cover information for Tongyu County, changes in the quantity, conversion types, and spatial distribution of saline-alkali land during three periods: 1989, 2000, and 2004, are analyzed. The results indicate a significant increase in the area of saline-alkali land from 1989 to 2000, while the rate of increase slowed down from 2000 to 2004, resulting in a decrease in area. In terms of land use type conversion, the main sources of saline-alkali land in the area are the degradation of grasslands and the shrinkage of water bodies and wetlands. Regarding the spatial distribution of changes, the overall trend of land use intensity is higher in the east than in the west, higher in the south than in the north, and higher in plains than in low-lying areas. The study clarifies the current situation of land salinization in Tongyu County, Jilin Province, and its changes over the past 16 years, providing essential baseline data and background information for research on the spatial distribution patterns of land salinization under different environmental contexts. (3) Combining laboratory data from Tongyu County, a correlation analysis is conducted between the characteristic parameters of land salinization and soluble salts, along with the corresponding scatter plots. Soluble salt indicators (HCO3⁻, CO3²⁻, Na⁺) that have a high correlation with land salinization characteristic parameters (total salt content, pH, alkalinity, residual sodium carbonate, and sodium adsorption ratio) are selected to create an indicator system that aligns with the characteristics of land salinization in Tongyu County. Using the contour mapping function of Surfer software, spatial distribution maps for each indicator are created, and their distribution is analyzed in conjunction with the townships of Tongyu County: eight indicators show higher concentrations mainly in Yangjing, Sugongtuo, Zhanyu, Shuanggang, and Sijingzi and their surrounding areas. With the aid of ARCGIS spatial data fusion technology, a spatial distribution map of land salinization levels is produced, analyzing the spatial distribution patterns of land salinization in Tongyu County: the salinization level gradually intensifies from west to east, with severe salinization observed in Sijingzi and Shuanggang in the north and most townships in the south. After extracting the distribution area of land salinization in Tongyu County using ARCGIS 9.0 and excluding water body areas, the area of lightly salinized land is 2.64×10⁸ hm², accounting for 3.13% of the total land area; the area of moderately salinized land is 4.45×10⁹ hm², accounting for 51.89% of the total land area; and the areas of severely and extremely severely salinized land account for 41.94% and 2.19% of the total land area, respectively. A brief analysis of the vertical distribution patterns of land salinization is also conducted. (4) Utilizing the GIS spatial overlay function and combining satellite remote sensing interpretation results of saline-alkali land in Tongyu County from 1989, 2000, and 2004, the interpretation maps of saline-alkali land are spatially overlaid with soil and landform type maps to study the spatial distribution patterns of land salinization over a long time series. The results indicate that between 1989 and 2004, saline-alkali land in Tongyu County is primarily distributed among soil units such as light black calcareous soil, saline soil, alkaline soil, and sandy soil. Field investigations reveal that saline-alkali soil is mostly found in low-lying areas between sand ridges, in catchment areas, and around waterlogged areas, primarily concentrated in river low terraces, river floodplains, and lake accumulation plains. The study finds that the spatial distribution of land salinization is closely related to the spatial distribution of these environmental background factors, with the spatial differentiation and organic combination of various environmental background factors jointly constraining the spatial distribution of saline-alkali land. (5) The analysis of factors influencing the quantity and distribution of saline-alkali land indicates that the main factors affecting land salinization include geological factors, climatic factors, and socio-economic factors. Landform types, soil types, temperature, and precipitation are fundamental controls on the distribution of saline-alkali land; while land use and management practices such as land reclamation, cultivation, overgrazing, and agricultural management measures are the inducements leading to land salinization.

From 1970 to 1985, the area of mildly saline-alkaline land in the study area increased; from 1985 to 2000, the area of severely saline-alkaline land significantly increased by 2887.45 km², while the area of lakes decreased by 1104.14 km², and the area of mildly saline-alkaline land decreased by 2903.12 km². Overall data indicates an increasing trend in the degree of salinization in the study area. From 1970 to 2000, the density of mildly saline-alkaline patches in western Jilin Province gradually decreased, while the density of moderately and severely saline-alkaline patches gradually increased. The density of potential saline-alkaline land and lake patches showed a trend of first increasing and then decreasing. In general, over the past 30 years, the fragmentation index of saline-alkaline land in western Jilin Province has decreased, while the degree of salinization has increased. According to the statistical values of the diversity index and evenness index of saline-alkaline land in the study area, the values in 1985 were higher than those in 1970, but gradually decreased by 2000. This is attributed to the relatively humid climate and increased precipitation around 1985, which reduced the degree of land salinization; however, influenced by factors such as climate, precipitation, and evaporation, the degree of salinization gradually increased in the subsequent years.

Using remote sensing and geographic information system technology, visual interpretation of land salinization in the western region of Jilin Province from 1970 to 2000 was conducted based on three periods of remote sensing images. The results indicate significant changes in land salinization over the 30 years: the area of saline-alkali land increased, while the area of lakes decreased. The area of severely saline-alkali land grew from 6,805.34 km² in 1970 to 9,211.98 km² in 2000, and the area of moderately saline-alkali land increased from 8,529.14 km² in 1970 to 9,448.76 km² in 2000. The area of lakes decreased from 2,735.93 km² in 1970 to 1,047.38 km² in 2000. A transfer matrix was used to analyze the dynamic changes in land salinization. Between 1970 and 1985, lakes converted into 50.01 km² of lightly saline-alkali land, 1,009.75 km² of moderately saline-alkali land, 311.92 km² of severely saline-alkali land, and 303.49 km² of potential saline-alkali land; moderately saline-alkali land converted into 686.43 km² of severely saline-alkali land. From 1985 to 2000, potential saline-alkali land converted into 1,937.68 km² of lightly saline-alkali land, 3,011.07 km² of moderately saline-alkali land, and 2,152.38 km² of severely saline-alkali land. The results show that the area of conversion between different land types is considerable and frequent, with lightly saline-alkali land transitioning to moderate and severe saline-alkali land, while the conversion from saline-alkali land to non-saline-alkali land is very challenging. An analysis using landscape pattern indices revealed a decrease in fragmentation index, indicating an increase in fragmentation within the study area. The degree of land salinization in western Jilin Province is continuously intensifying, primarily characterized by the salinization of grasslands, with saline-alkali land mainly distributed at the junction of Tongyu and Da'an. Due to overgrazing and water resource pollution, grassland degradation has occurred, leading to many pastures losing their utility. The increasing severity and quantity of land salinization, along with its accelerated growth rate, suggest that land salinization results from the combined effects of natural and anthropogenic factors. Natural factors primarily include landform type, soil type, temperature, and precipitation, while anthropogenic factors mainly involve unreasonable human use of land resources. There are significant regional differences in the dynamic changes of land salinization in western Jilin Province, with the fastest growth rate of saline-alkali land occurring in Da'an. Potential desertification areas are mainly located in low-lying regions, where the low terrain is prone to waterlogging, leading to salinization.

Soil salinization is a natural disaster characterized by excessive levels of chlorides, sulfates, and carbonates in the soil, with these salts accumulating at the surface. Salinization is influenced by various factors such as land surface, local precipitation, temperature, and evaporation rates. However, the presence of a large number of salt-tolerant plants in a region indicates that salinization is occurring there. Typical salt-tolerant plants include Salix triandra, Caragana korshinskii, Haloxylon ammodendron, and Ziziphus jujuba. Salinized land can be categorized into types such as arable land, grassland, and wetland: (1) Arable land: refers to areas where salinization is not too severe, or where saline-alkali land has been artificially modified for cultivation. Mildly salinized land can support crops like corn, wheat, soybeans, and sorghum, while moderately salinized land can grow sunflowers and beets. (2) Grassland: refers to herbaceous plants growing on saline-alkali land, which is the most widely distributed type among all saline-alkali land types. Mildly salinized land features species like Leymus chinensis and Stipa capillata, while moderately salinized land includes species such as Puccinellia tenuiflora and Artemisia halodendron. (3) Wetland: refers to flat, low-lying areas with poor drainage that are often damp, with seasonal or perennial water accumulation, where surface salts are concentrated, supporting the growth of salt-tolerant and moisture-loving plants. Salinized wetlands are mainly divided into saline-alkaline lakes and saline-alkaline marshes.

This chapter divides the land salinization of the Songnen Plain through soil remote sensing inversion and land use remote sensing interpretation, obtaining the area and spatial distribution of saline-alkali land in the study area from 2000 to 2020. The main conclusions are as follows: (1) From 2000 to 2020, the total area of land salinization in the Songnen Plain showed a decreasing trend, with a reduction rate of 13.67 km²/5a, indicating that the degree of salinization has further slowed down; the area of lightly salinized land showed an increasing trend, with an increase rate of 17.023 km²/5a, while the areas of moderately and severely salinized land showed decreasing trends, with reduction rates of 20.431 km²/5a and 10.261 km²/5a, respectively. Comprehensive analysis indicates that the continuous decrease in the area of moderately and severely salinized land has led to a reduction in the total area of salinized land. (2) There are significant differences in the dynamics of land salinization in the Songnen Plain; the area of lightly salinized land generally shows an increasing trend, while the areas of moderately and severely salinized land show decreasing trends. The growth rate of lightly salinized land in the later period gradually slowed down, with a slight increase in area; the rate of change in moderately salinized land in the later period first increased and then decreased, with the decrease rate being less than the average decrease rate, indicating a rebound phenomenon in salinized land, which requires relevant measures for prevention and control; the rate of severely salinized land decreased year by year, and the salinization situation improved annually. (3) There is mutual transformation among saline-alkali lands in the Songnen Plain. From 2000 to 2010, the area of positive transformation of lightly salinized land was less than that of reverse transformation, leading to further expansion of lightly salinized land, primarily converting to cultivated land and severely salinized land. From 2010 to 2020, the area of positive transformation of lightly salinized land was again less than that of reverse transformation, exacerbating the degree of salinization; the area of reverse transformation of moderately salinized land was greater than that of positive transformation, intensifying salinization, with the main conversion types being grassland, lightly salinized land, and severely salinized land; the area of positive transformation of severely salinized land was greater than that of reverse transformation, indicating a decrease in the degree of salinization. (4) From 2000 to 2010, the area of land salinization in the Songnen Plain showed a decreasing trend, with the degree of salinization gradually alleviating, resulting in a reduction of 134.64 km² in salinized land, primarily due to the decrease in moderately salinized land. The degree of salinization in northern Daqing City decreased, while it intensified in southern Daqing City and southern Suifenhe; from 2010 to 2020, the area of land salinization in the Songnen Plain showed an increasing trend, with the degree of salinization gradually worsening, resulting in an increase of 74.88 km² in salinized land, primarily due to the increase in lightly salinized land. The degree of salinization decreased in the central and northern parts of Daqing City and southern Suifenhe City, while it intensified in southern Daqing City, northern Suifenhe City, and southern Qiqihar City. (5) In the study area, the total area of saline-alkali land in Qiqihar City and Suifenhe City shows an increasing trend, with worsening salinization; the total area of land salinization and severely salinized land in Daqing City is greater than in the other three administrative regions, indicating a large base of salinized land. However, in recent years, the area of land salinization in Daqing City showed a turning point in 2015, with the total area of saline-alkali land showing a decreasing trend, indicating a significant improvement in the degree of land salinization in Daqing; the salinized land in Harbin City is gradually improving.

(1) The main driving factors for the change in the area of land salinization and alkalization in the Songnen Plain are social economy and agricultural and pastoral development, while climate change also has a certain impact on land salinization and alkalization. (2) The precipitation and total heavy rainfall in the spring and summer of the Songnen Plain generally show an increasing trend, while the evaporation in spring and summer shows a decreasing trend, which slows down the phenomenon of "returning salt" in spring and enhances the phenomenon of "salt removal" in summer. At the same time, the depth of groundwater is generally showing a slow trend of becoming shallower. (3) The long-term agricultural development of the Songnen Plain will gradually increase the irrigated area based on the river and lake connectivity projects in the region. Water is the key factor in controlling salinization and alkalization; as the irrigated area increases, the area of land salinization and alkalization in the Songnen Plain will show a gradual decreasing trend. Therefore, it is necessary to adopt localized measures, establish comprehensive management strategies, improve soil quality, and avoid the generation of secondary salinization and alkalization.
